question: [File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: IdLib.toId / Midnight.touchMarket] Can an unprivileged market creator, by pre-deploying a contract at the CREATE2 address computed by IdLib.toId (using the same SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(market), INITIAL_CHAIN_ID, and midnight address) before touchMarket is called, cause storeInCode to return address(0) and revert market creation, permanently blocking a specific market ID and freezing any lender or borrower who pre-signed offers for that market? Preconditions: attacker knows the target market params and INITIAL_CHAIN_ID; attacker deploys any contract at address(uint160(uint256(keccak256(abi.encodePacked(uint8(0xff), midnight, INITIAL_CHAIN_ID, keccak256(abi.encodePacked(SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(market)))))))) before touchMarket. Call sequence: attacker.deploy(create2Address) -> victim.touchMarket(market) -> revert(SStore2DeploymentFailed). Invariant tested: market creation must succeed for any valid market params if no prior market exists at that ID. Scoped impact: fund freeze; valid lenders and borrowers cannot enter the market. Proof idea: fuzz market params, pre-deploy at computed create2 address, assert touchMarket reverts.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: IdLib.toId / Midnight.toMarket] Can an unprivileged attacker, by constructing two distinct Market structs whose abi.encode representations differ only in dynamic array padding or zero-value trailing fields, cause IdLib.toId to produce the same bytes32 id for both markets, allowing accounting state (totalUnits, withdrawable, lossFactor) from one market to be shared with the other, enabling credit units in the second market to redeem assets funded by repayments in the first? Preconditions: attacker finds or constructs two Market structs with identical keccak256(abi.encodePacked(SSTORE2_PREFIX, abi.encode(market))); both markets have different maturity or loanToken. Call sequence: touchMarket(market1) -> take/repay on market1 -> touchMarket(market2) skips creation -> withdraw on market2 drains market1 withdrawable. Invariant tested: distinct Market structs must produce distinct ids. Scoped impact: direct loss; lenders of market2 redeem assets funded by market1 repayments. Proof idea: differential fuzz abi.encode(market1) vs abi.encode(market2) and assert toId produces distinct hashes.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: IdLib.toId / Midnight.toMarket] Can an unprivileged attacker, after a hard fork that changes block.chainid while INITIAL_CHAIN_ID remains fixed at the pre-fork value, create a new market on the forked chain with the same params as an existing pre-fork market, causing toId to return the same id on both chains, and then use the forked chain's market accounting (which starts fresh) to redeem credit units against withdrawable that was funded by pre-fork repayments replayed on the fork? Preconditions: hard fork changes block.chainid; INITIAL_CHAIN_ID is the pre-fork chainId; attacker replays repay() calls on the fork to inflate withdrawable; attacker holds credit from pre-fork. Call sequence: fork -> repay(market, units, borrower) on fork -> withdraw(market, units, attacker) on fork. Invariant tested: post-fork accounting must not allow credit from one chain to redeem assets funded on another. Scoped impact: direct loss; attacker redeems more assets than economically owed on the forked chain. Proof idea: simulate fork by deploying Midnight with same INITIAL_CHAIN_ID, replay repay, assert withdraw drains balance.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.toMarket / IdLib.toId] Can an unprivileged attacker, by calling toMarket(id) with an id whose lower 20 bytes correspond to a contract address that has been self-destructed and redeployed (via CREATE2 with different initcode) with a different abi.encode(Market) payload, cause toMarket to return a Market struct with a different maturity, loanToken, or collateralParams than the one used at market creation, allowing updatePositionView to compute incorrect fee accrual and enabling credit units to redeem more assets than owed? Preconditions: EIP-6780 or pre-Cancun chain allows SELFDESTRUCT; attacker controls a contract at the SSTORE2 create2 address; attacker redeploys with modified market params. Call sequence: selfdestruct(create2Address) -> redeploy with modified Market -> toMarket(id) returns wrong params -> updatePositionView uses wrong maturity -> withdraw redeems excess. Invariant tested: toMarket must always return the exact Market used at touchMarket time. Scoped impact: direct loss; credit redeems more than economically owed due to wrong maturity in fee accrual. Proof idea: on pre-Cancun fork, selfdestruct and redeploy SSTORE2 contract, assert toMarket returns modified params.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView / _updatePosition] Can an unprivileged lender, by calling _updatePosition with a Market memory struct whose maturity field differs from the one stored in the SSTORE2 contract (since Market is passed as memory and not re-read from toMarket), cause updatePositionView to compute accrualEnd = min(block.timestamp, attacker_maturity) with a manipulated maturity, resulting in fee = 0 (if attacker passes maturity = block.timestamp) and thus preserving more credit than economically owed, allowing withdraw to redeem excess assets? Preconditions: attacker constructs a Market memory struct with same loanToken/collateralParams but different maturity; attacker calls updatePosition(manipulatedMarket, user) directly; the id computed by toId(manipulatedMarket) must match the real market id. Call sequence: updatePosition(manipulatedMarket, lender) -> _updatePosition uses wrong maturity -> fee = 0 -> credit not reduced -> withdraw(realMarket, credit, lender). Invariant tested: fee accrual must use the maturity stored at market creation, not caller-supplied maturity. Scoped impact: direct loss; lender avoids continuous fee deduction and redeems full credit. Proof idea: fuzz maturity in Market memory passed to updatePosition, assert accruedFee matches expected value from stored maturity.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the formula fee = postSlashPendingFee.mulDivDown(accrualEnd - _lastAccrual, market.maturity - _lastAccrual) when _lastAccrual is set to a value very close to market.maturity (e.g., market.maturity - 1), causing the denominator market.maturity - _lastAccrual = 1 and the numerator accrualEnd - _lastAccrual = 1, so fee = postSlashPendingFee, fully accruing the pending fee in a single block, and then immediately calling withdraw to redeem credit - pendingFee assets, which may be less than the economically owed amount if the fee was supposed to accrue gradually? Preconditions: lender has credit with nonzero pendingFee; lastAccrual is set to market.maturity - 1 (e.g., by calling _updatePosition at that timestamp); block.timestamp >= market.maturity. Call sequence: warp(maturity - 1) -> _updatePosition(market, lender) sets lastAccrual = maturity - 1 -> warp(maturity) -> withdraw(market, credit, lender) -> updatePositionView accrues full pendingFee. Invariant tested: fee accrual must be proportional to elapsed time; no single-block full accrual. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; fee accrual timing can be gamed to minimize fee paid. Proof idea: fuzz lastAccrual near maturity, assert fee == pendingFee * (accrualEnd - lastAccrual) / (maturity - lastAccrual).

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.withdraw / _updatePosition] Can an unprivileged lender, by calling withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) with a Market memory struct that has the same id as the real market (same loanToken, collateralParams, maturity, etc.) but a different rcfThreshold or enterGate, cause _updatePosition to use the wrong market params for fee accrual (specifically market.maturity), resulting in incorrect credit computation and allowing the lender to withdraw more loan tokens than their economically correct post-fee credit? Preconditions: attacker constructs Market memory with same core fields but different rcfThreshold; toId(manipulatedMarket) == toId(realMarket) because rcfThreshold is included in abi.encode(market). Call sequence: withdraw(manipulatedMarket, units, lender, receiver). Invariant tested: withdraw must use the exact market params stored at creation for all accounting. Scoped impact: direct loss if rcfThreshold affects id computation; accounting corruption otherwise. Proof idea: assert toId(market1) != toId(market2) for any single-field difference in Market struct.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.repay / withdraw] Can an unprivileged borrower, by using the IRepayCallback interface to reenter withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver) inside onRepay before the safeTransferFrom in repay completes, exploit the fact that repay increments marketState[id].withdrawable += units before pulling tokens, allowing the lender to withdraw units loan tokens from the contract's pre-existing balance before the repayment tokens arrive, effectively double-spending the withdrawable balance? Preconditions: contract has pre-existing withdrawable balance >= units from prior repayments; lender has credit >= units; borrower has debt >= units; borrower uses a callback contract as payer. Call sequence: repay(market, units, borrower, callback, data) -> withdrawable += units -> onRepay reenters withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver) -> withdrawable -= units, safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) -> repay continues safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, units). Invariant tested: withdrawable must not be spendable before the corresponding tokens are deposited. Scoped impact: direct loss; lender receives tokens before borrower deposits them, draining prior repayments. Proof idea: PoC with malicious IRepayCallback that reenters withdraw, assert contract balance goes negative or prior lender cannot withdraw.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take / withdraw] Can an unprivileged taker, by using the IBuyCallback interface to reenter withdraw(market, units, buyer, receiver) inside onBuy after take has already incremented buyerPos.credit and before the safeTransferFrom pulls buyerAssets, allow the buyer to withdraw credit units as loan tokens from the contract's pre-existing withdrawable balance without the buyer having paid buyerAssets yet, resulting in the buyer receiving assets for free? Preconditions: contract has withdrawable >= units from prior repayments; buyer has credit >= units after take's credit update; buyer's callback reenters withdraw. Call sequence: take(offer, ...) -> buyerPos.credit += buyerCreditIncrease -> onBuy reenters withdraw(market, units, buyer, receiver) -> withdrawable -= units, safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) -> take continues safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, this, buyerAssets - sellerAssets). Invariant tested: credit must not be withdrawable until the corresponding assets have been deposited. Scoped impact: direct loss; buyer receives loan tokens without paying. Proof idea: PoC with malicious IBuyCallback that reenters withdraw, assert contract balance decreases without corresponding deposit.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take / repay] Can an unprivileged taker, by using the ISellCallback interface to reenter repay(market, units, seller, address(0), '') inside onSell after take has already incremented sellerPos.debt, cause the seller's debt to be repaid (withdrawable += units) before the seller has actually transferred any tokens, and then immediately withdraw those units, effectively creating credit redeemable for assets without any economic backing? Preconditions: seller has debt after take's debt update; seller's callback reenters repay with units <= seller's new debt; contract has no prior withdrawable. Call sequence: take(offer, ...) -> sellerPos.debt += sellerDebtIncrease -> onSell reenters repay(market, units, seller, address(0), '') -> withdrawable += units -> onSell returns -> take completes -> withdraw(market, units, lender). Invariant tested: withdrawable must only increase when actual loan tokens are deposited. Scoped impact: direct loss; lender redeems assets that were never deposited. Proof idea: PoC with malicious ISellCallback that reenters repay, assert withdrawable exceeds actual token balance.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.claimContinuousFee / _updatePosition] Can an unprivileged lender exploit a rounding discrepancy between the accruedFee computed in updatePositionView (rounded down via mulDivDown) and the continuousFeeCredit accumulated over many _updatePosition calls, causing continuousFeeCredit to exceed the sum of all pendingFee reductions across all lenders, allowing the fee claimer to call claimContinuousFee with an amount that reduces withdrawable below what remaining lenders can collectively withdraw, making some lenders unable to redeem their full credit? Preconditions: market has many lenders with small pendingFee; _updatePosition called many times with rounding-favorable timestamps; continuousFeeCredit accumulates rounding surplus. Call sequence: N * _updatePosition(market, lender_i) -> continuousFeeCredit > sum(pendingFee_i reductions) -> claimContinuousFee(market, continuousFeeCredit, receiver) -> withdrawable < sum(credit_i). Invariant tested: sum(credit_i) <= withdrawable + continuousFeeCredit at all times. Scoped impact: fund freeze; some lenders cannot withdraw their full credit. Proof idea: fuzz N lenders and timestamps, assert withdrawable >= sum(credit_i) after claimContinuousFee.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate / updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged liquidator, by calling liquidate with badDebt > 0 when totalUnits is very small (e.g., totalUnits = 1), cause the lossFactor update formula lossFactor = type(uint128).max - (type(uint128).max - _lossFactor).mulDivDown(_totalUnits - badDebt, _totalUnits) to set lossFactor = type(uint128).max, wiping all lender credit to 0 via updatePositionView, while withdrawable remains nonzero (funded by prior repayments), leaving assets permanently stuck in the contract and unredeemable by any lender? Preconditions: market has totalUnits = 1, withdrawable > 0 from prior repayments; borrower has debt = 1 with no collateral (bad debt). Call sequence: repay(market, N, borrower) -> withdrawable = N -> liquidate(market, 0, 0, 0, borrower, false, ...) -> badDebt = 1 -> lossFactor = type(uint128).max -> all lenders' credit -> 0 -> withdraw reverts. Invariant tested: if withdrawable > 0, at least one lender must be able to withdraw. Scoped impact: fund freeze; withdrawable assets permanently locked. Proof idea: fuzz totalUnits near 1, assert withdrawable is reachable after lossFactor update.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator, by calling liquidate twice on the same borrower with badDebt > 0 in both calls (first call realizes partial bad debt, second call realizes remaining bad debt), cause the lossFactor to be updated twice in a way that over-reduces lender credit beyond the actual bad debt amount, due to the multiplicative nature of the lossFactor formula, resulting in lenders losing more credit than the total bad debt? Preconditions: borrower has debt D with collateral worth < D/maxLif; first liquidation realizes badDebt1 < D; second liquidation realizes badDebt2 = remaining debt. Call sequence: liquidate(market, collatIdx, 0, 0, borrower, false, ...) -> lossFactor updated for badDebt1 -> liquidate(market, collatIdx, 0, 0, borrower, false, ...) -> lossFactor updated for badDebt2. Invariant tested: total credit reduction across all lenders must equal total bad debt, not more. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; lenders lose more than the bad debt amount. Proof idea: fuzz badDebt1 and badDebt2, assert sum(credit_i_before - credit_i_after) == badDebt1 + badDebt2.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take at exactly block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity (where the check block.timestamp <= offer.market.maturity passes), create new seller debt (sellerDebtIncrease > 0) with timeToMaturity = 0, resulting in buyerPendingFeeIncrease = 0 (no continuous fee charged), and then immediately post-maturity the seller's debt is liquidatable, allowing the buyer to hold credit with zero pending fee obligation while the seller's debt is liquidated and bad debt is socialized, enabling the buyer to redeem full credit without the fee cost that would have been charged for any positive timeToMaturity? Preconditions: block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity; offer is a sell offer with sellerDebtIncrease > 0; seller has no prior credit. Call sequence: warp(maturity) -> take(offer, ..., units) -> sellerDebtIncrease > 0, buyerPendingFeeIncrease = 0 -> warp(maturity + 1) -> liquidate(market, ..., borrower, true, ...) -> buyer withdraws full credit. Invariant tested: credit created at maturity must bear the same fee obligations as credit created just before maturity. Scoped impact: direct loss; buyer redeems full credit without continuous fee deduction. Proof idea: compare pendingFee for credit created at maturity-1 vs maturity, assert proportional fee.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take / settlementFee] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take at a timestamp that falls exactly on a settlement fee breakpoint boundary (e.g., timeToMaturity = 1 day, 7 days, 30 days, 90 days, 180 days, or 360 days), exploit the piecewise linear interpolation formula (feeLower * (end - timeToMaturity) + feeUpper * (timeToMaturity - start)) / (end - start) to obtain a settlement fee that rounds down to zero due to integer division, causing buyerAssets == sellerAssets and zero settlement fee being charged, allowing the buyer to acquire credit units at the seller's price without paying any protocol fee? Preconditions: timeToMaturity is exactly at a breakpoint; feeLower and feeUpper are set such that the interpolation rounds to 0. Call sequence: warp(maturity - breakpoint) -> take(offer, ...) -> settlementFee = 0 -> claimableSettlementFee unchanged -> buyer gets credit at seller price. Invariant tested: settlement fee must be nonzero for all timeToMaturity > 0 when fee breakpoints are nonzero. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; protocol loses settlement fee revenue. Proof idea: fuzz timeToMaturity at all breakpoints, assert settlementFee >= min(feeLower, feeUpper) when both are nonzero.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take with a sell offer where offerPrice is very close to WAD (e.g., tick = MAX_TICK), causing buyerPrice = offerPrice + settlementFee > WAD, result in buyerAssets = units.mulDivUp(buyerPrice, WAD) > units, where the buyer pays more than units assets but only receives units credit, and then the buyer can only withdraw units assets via withdraw(), meaning the buyer overpays and the excess (buyerAssets - units) is permanently locked in the contract as neither withdrawable nor claimableSettlementFee? Preconditions: sell offer at tick = MAX_TICK; settlementFee > 0; buyerPrice > WAD. Call sequence: take(sellOffer, ..., units) -> buyerAssets > units -> claimableSettlementFee += buyerAssets - sellerAssets -> withdrawable unchanged -> buyer's credit = units -> withdraw(market, units, buyer) -> buyer gets units, not buyerAssets. Invariant tested: all buyer-paid assets must be recoverable as either credit redemption, settlement fee, or seller payment. Scoped impact: fund freeze; excess buyer payment permanently locked. Proof idea: fuzz tick near MAX_TICK and settlementFee, assert buyerAssets == sellerAssets + claimableSettlementFee_increase + withdrawable_increase.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take / _updatePosition] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take where the buyer has existing credit (hasCredit returns true) and _updatePosition is called for the buyer, cause the buyer's credit to be slashed by the current lossFactor before the new credit is added, resulting in the buyer receiving fewer credit units than expected (buyerCreditIncrease is computed after slashing), while the buyer still pays the full buyerAssets, effectively overpaying for the credit received? Preconditions: market has lossFactor > 0; buyer has existing credit; take increases buyer's credit. Call sequence: liquidate(market, ...) -> lossFactor > 0 -> take(buyOffer, ...) -> _updatePosition(market, id, buyer) slashes credit -> buyerCreditIncrease = zeroFloorSub(units, buyerPos.debt) uses slashed debt -> buyer pays buyerAssets but gets fewer credit units. Invariant tested: buyer must receive exactly units credit increase for units paid at the offer price. Scoped impact: direct loss to buyer; buyer overpays for credit. Proof idea: fuzz lossFactor and buyer's prior credit, assert credit_after - credit_before == buyerCreditIncrease.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.withdraw] Can an unprivileged lender, by calling withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) with units = 0, trigger a no-op that still calls _updatePosition (which accrues fees and updates lastAccrual to block.timestamp), allowing the lender to reset their lastAccrual to the current timestamp without withdrawing anything, effectively stopping future fee accrual for the remainder of the market's life if called just before maturity, and then withdraw full credit post-maturity without the expected fee deduction? Preconditions: lender has credit with nonzero pendingFee; block.timestamp is close to market.maturity; lender calls withdraw with units = 0. Call sequence: warp(maturity - 1) -> withdraw(market, 0, lender, receiver) -> _updatePosition sets lastAccrual = maturity - 1, accrues fee for period [lastAccrual_old, maturity-1] -> warp(maturity) -> withdraw(market, credit, lender) -> fee = pendingFee * 1 / 1 = pendingFee (full remaining fee). Invariant tested: zero-unit withdraw must not allow fee avoidance. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; lender avoids partial fee. Proof idea: compare total fee paid with and without zero-unit withdraw call near maturity.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged lender exploit the formula postSlashPendingFee = _pendingFee - _pendingFee.mulDivUp(credit - postSlashCredit, credit) where mulDivUp rounds up, causing postSlashPendingFee to be slightly less than the economically correct value, and then the fee = postSlashPendingFee.mulDivDown(accrualEnd - _lastAccrual, market.maturity - _lastAccrual) to also round down, resulting in the lender's credit being reduced by less than the correct fee amount, allowing the lender to withdraw slightly more assets than economically owed? Preconditions: market has lossFactor > 0; lender has credit and pendingFee; multiple rounding-favorable values for credit, pendingFee, and lossFactor. Call sequence: liquidate(market, ...) -> lossFactor > 0 -> warp(maturity) -> withdraw(market, credit, lender) -> updatePositionView computes fee with double rounding down -> lender withdraws credit - fee_rounded_down. Invariant tested: sum of all lender withdrawals must not exceed total repaid assets minus fees. Scoped impact: direct loss; lenders collectively withdraw more than deposited. Proof idea: fuzz credit, pendingFee, lossFactor, assert sum(withdrawals) <= withdrawable_initial.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator, by calling liquidate with postMaturityMode = true at block.timestamp = market.maturity + 1 (one second after maturity), when the LIF ramp formula lif = WAD + (maxLif - WAD) * 1 / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF rounds down to WAD (since TIME_TO_MAX_LIF = 900 seconds and maxLif - WAD may be small), cause the liquidator to seize collateral at LIF = WAD (no incentive), and then the bad debt realization formula to incorrectly compute badDebt using maxLif instead of the actual lif = WAD, causing lenders to lose more credit than the actual bad debt? Preconditions: block.timestamp = market.maturity + 1; postMaturityMode = true; lif rounds to WAD; badDebt computed with maxLif. Call sequence: warp(maturity + 1) -> liquidate(market, collatIdx, seizedAssets, 0, borrower, true, ...) -> lif = WAD -> seize at par -> badDebt uses maxLif -> lenders lose more than actual bad debt. Invariant tested: bad debt realization must use the same LIF as the liquidation incentive computation. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; lenders lose more credit than the actual bad debt. Proof idea: fuzz timestamp in [maturity, maturity + TIME_TO_MAX_LIF], assert badDebt == debt - collateral_value_at_lif.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take with a buy offer where the maker has existing debt (buyerPos.debt > 0) and units <= buyerPos.debt, cause buyerCreditIncrease = 0 and the buyer's debt to decrease by units, while the seller receives sellerAssets and the buyer pays buyerAssets, but totalUnits is unchanged (since buyerCreditIncrease = 0 and sellerCreditDecrease = 0 if seller has no credit), meaning the buyer reduces debt without any corresponding reduction in withdrawable, creating a state where totalUnits > withdrawable + outstanding_debt? Preconditions: buyer has debt > 0; units <= buyer's debt; seller has no credit; offer is a buy offer. Call sequence: take(buyOffer, ..., units) -> buyerCreditIncrease = 0 -> buyerPos.debt -= units -> totalUnits unchanged -> withdrawable unchanged. Invariant tested: totalUnits must equal sum(credit_i) + continuousFeeCredit at all times. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; totalUnits desync from actual credit. Proof idea: fuzz buyer debt and units, assert totalUnits == sum(all credit) + continuousFeeCredit after take.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a take with a sell offer where the seller has existing credit (sellerPos.credit > 0) and units <= sellerPos.credit, cause sellerCreditDecrease = units and totalUnits to decrease by units, while withdrawable stays the same, creating a state where withdrawable > totalUnits, and then a subsequent lender can withdraw more than their proportional share of the remaining withdrawable because withdraw() is 1:1 and doesn't check withdrawable/totalUnits ratio? Preconditions: seller has credit > 0; units <= seller's credit; market has withdrawable > 0 from prior repayments; another lender has credit. Call sequence: take(sellOffer, ..., units) -> sellerCreditDecrease = units -> totalUnits -= units -> withdrawable unchanged -> other_lender.withdraw(market, credit, other_lender) -> withdraws full credit even though withdrawable > totalUnits. Invariant tested: withdrawable must not exceed totalUnits after any operation. Scoped impact: direct loss; remaining lenders can withdraw more than their share. [File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.claimContinuousFee] Can an unprivileged fee claimer, by calling claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) with amount > continuousFeeCredit (which would underflow continuousFeeCredit) or amount > withdrawable (which would underflow withdrawable), cause an underflow revert, or alternatively, if amount == continuousFeeCredit but continuousFeeCredit was inflated by rounding in _updatePosition, allow the fee claimer to drain more from withdrawable than was actually accrued from lenders' pendingFee, leaving insufficient withdrawable for lenders to redeem their credit? Preconditions: continuousFeeCredit is inflated by rounding; fee claimer calls claimContinuousFee with amount = continuousFeeCredit. Call sequence: N * _updatePosition(market, lender_i) -> continuousFeeCredit inflated -> claimContinuousFee(market, continuousFeeCredit, receiver) -> withdrawable -= continuousFeeCredit -> some lenders cannot withdraw. Invariant tested: withdrawable - continuousFeeCredit >= sum(credit_i) at all times. Scoped impact: fund freeze; lenders cannot redeem full credit after fee claim. Proof idea: fuzz N lenders and accrual periods, assert withdrawable >= sum(credit_i) after claimContinuousFee.

[File: 'src/libraries/IdLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Market settlement or maturity logic flaw that allows credit/debt units to redeem for more assets than economically owed'] [Function: Midnight.take / Midnight.withdraw] Can an unprivileged taker, by executing a multicall that atomically calls take (creating credit for the buyer) and withdraw (redeeming that credit) in the same transaction, exploit the fact that take does not update withdrawable when creating new credit (withdrawable only increases via repay), causing the withdraw call to fail with underflow if withdrawable < units, or alternatively, if there is pre-existing withdrawable from prior repayments, allow the buyer to immediately redeem newly created credit against prior repayments without waiting for the corresponding debt to be repaid? Preconditions: market has withdrawable > 0 from prior repayments; buyer takes a buy offer creating new credit; buyer immediately withdraws in same multicall. Call sequence: multicall([take(buyOffer, ..., units), withdraw(market, units, buyer, receiver)]) -> buyer gets credit -> buyer withdraws against prior repayments. Invariant tested: newly created credit must not be immediately redeemable against prior repayments without corresponding debt repayment. Scoped impact: direct loss; buyer redeems assets funded by other borrowers' repayments. Proof idea: PoC multicall take+withdraw, assert withdrawable decreases only by repaid amounts.
